item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTF Nu-13 ("Rasczak's Roughnecks") is to maintain a provincial base

Did you mean "provisional"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They saw instances of 3035, which are roaches that mimic physical attributes of other lifeforms. In this case, the roaches had mimicked the attributes of humans. So, they were roach-like humans, yes (or human-like roaches).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…what did they see that WAS DEFINITELY A ROACH?

Like, a roach/human mixed breed? a human test subject? actually just a roach, but the person who saw it is freaking out?

Looks like the author deleted their account?! Will I never have an answer?!

(I posted this in the discussion of the expedition log itself as well…sorry if thats not proper but I REALLY want an answer. lol)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes me remember an old science fiction novel called "La planète des singes" regarding apes imitated and finally replaced human beings. The apes were not intelligent enough to make new inventions and their science level remained almost same from the time the incident happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this skip. It's got a good hook, a good narrative, and great execution. I'm not the biggest fan of the new exploration log, but I still like the main article enough to give it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's safe because they're trapped in an underground site with only one access point (which consists of an air-tight lock).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hardly seems safe anymore, wouldn't Euclid be more fitting for a bunch of giant bugs that mimic what they see to near perfection, with no actual concern for What they are mimicking?

item.title: Re: Typo?
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! — and fixed (re: the missing 'be').

Re: Colons and capitalization of independent clauses — I think it's one of those things where there's no precisely correct answer; it can go either way? Like the Oxford/Harvard comma.

item.title: Typo?
item.content:encoded:
I think I found a typo: in the third email, subject "My new research staff?", I think the clause "They've proven to very apt students" should read "They've proven to be very apt students". But I wasn't sure enough that it wasn't intentional to just change it.

Also, I don't think clauses following a colon should start with a capital letter. But that could just be a stylistic thing?

Those minor concerns aside, I really enjoy this. I found it creepy and disgusting and engaging. Good job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but feel like there's a dissonance between the description and the logs. The description keeps emphasizing how superficial the mimicry is, only offhandedly mentioning the mutating bit, while in the log they're suddenly capable of growing to human size and forming organic weaponry.

item.title: Oh yeah
item.content:encoded: Guess who just found a new favorite SCP…+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I immediately thought of Mimic as soon as I started reading this, and the feeling only increased as I continued. I'd honestly have been disappointed if The Great Hippo hadn't mentioned it, since the parallels are so strong. But I don't count that as a detriment, as this works well within the frame of the Foundation.

(I'll admit that Mimic and [especially] its sequels are terrible, terrible movies, but I love watching bad horror movies. Now I want to go watch it again.)

item.title: Re: Shouldn't this be rated as Euclid?
item.content:encoded:
Hey! Thanks for the comment! xD

Re: Point #1
– hide block

Object classes tend to refer to how difficult something is to contain — not its overall dangerousness. Since SCP-3035 is basically locked inside of an air-tight concrete bunker with only one access point (that the Foundation controls), it's safe — because if the Foundation wanted to, they could just plaster the entrance with concrete, walk away, and SCP-3035 would never get out.

Re: Point #2
– hide block

As a rule, I only redact/censor things if I think the Foundation would want them to be hidden for some reason. There's no reason the Foundation would really mind people knowing the names of the doctors involved in this SCP, so they didn't censor them.

item.title: Shouldn't this be rated as Euclid?
item.content:encoded:
Really enjoyed the SCP! A couple of thoughts:
1.After reading through the Dr logs and the Moon team logs I don't see how they can be rated as safe

2. Shouldn't the the Dr names in the emails be redacted since they aren't code names like the Moon team?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me, the idea wasn't very interesting. The execution & how you developed it [examples removed for brevity] was done very well & is what made this interesting & fun to read :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see your Starship Troopers reference and being the massive 'old sci-fi nerd' I am that tickled me pink. :>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that's… kind of embarrassing, I'm shocked I missed that. You're absolutely right; fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only issue with the article is the time; the e-mails have a timestamp of the early 80's which does not really fit some if the mannerisms or technology of the time, I mean yes e-mail has existed since the 70's but the concept of modern memes did not and the .jpg format did not become a thing until 1992 (the group that developed it formed in 1986).

Basically the only actual change I'd suggest is changing the dates from 1982 to 2002.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, isn't the solution sending one guy in to kill one bug and waiting until they all mimic that and kill each other?

I had the same thought, courtesy of Daniel Way: http://imgur.com/c4HAz

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, this is amazing and I love it. Seems from the comments like you fixed a lot of things. Also I must have +1 it at the same time as someone else because it went from 99 to 101.
Anyway, I only have one issue with this, and that is the bugs didn't seem to be able to understand the idea of killing a different being until suddenly they were murdering everyone?

Their form of mimicry was 'someone killed one of us, now lets us kill one of us' but that means that unless they see a human murder another human, or [something/a human] murder something else, they really shouldn't be mimicking, it right?

Like, isn't the solution sending one guy in to kill one bug and waiting until they all mimic that and kill each other? —> That's what it seems like should logically follow from your article. Though you said you're going to add some exploration logs so maybe that will explain it.

Heh, I hope I explained it well. It obviously wasn't bad enough for a no-vote but it was notable enough for me to bother commenting.
Either way, *awesome* SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wish I could see caseofthemondays.jpg irl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I decided to add an exploration log (as a supplemental, additional article). Hopefully this doesn't break the article for anyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly, the only reason they haven't bug-bombed the place is because there's a possibility there are still survivors trying to get out. They believe this on account of the fact that organs and body parts are still emerging from Entry Point B, which implies there are still survivors being dissected by the bugs.

Of course, that implies that maybe they should just bug-bomb the place and perform a mercy-killing, but I imagine the decision is tied up in committee.

(I'm thinking of doing an exploration log at some point, but if I do, it'll probably be on a different page that will just be linked here)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I was a little worried I didn't make that clear enough (what was leaving Entry Point B, I mean); I'm glad to receive confirmation that someone got it!

I've been debating returning to this to add a tale-ish exploration log that's secondary to the document (not on the page itself); maybe I will after some thought — I have a pretty good idea of what happened, but it's sometimes a bit of a challenge for me to put it down on paper.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thoroughly enjoyed this SCP; as you did in SCP-3034, there is just the right amount left to the imagination - something which is evident in the final email which I particularly liked.
Personally, I really enjoyed the email exchange; for me it made the article. It wasn't until I read it that I put two and two together and realised exactly what was leaving Entry Point B. Jeepers.

I do agree with what other users have said with regards to an Exploration Log. I too would like to know what other anomalies were previously at Site-573 and if the bugs ultimately began mimicking them. I understand the Foundation motto is 'Secure, Contain, Protect', but have other anomalies at the site been compromised in that process?

All in all, a fantastically disturbing SCP.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think an exploration log would be really great for this. Something to show how the bugs were able to compromise a Foundation site, since as others have mentioned that is a bit of a sticking point.

Also, why can't they just bug bomb the place? Flood the site with gas through the tube system and kill everything in there. They've already written off any survivors in the site anyway, and it wouldn't be unprecedented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've actually been debating adding a (separately stored) exploration log performed by a drone to show just how extensively these things have entrenched themselves in the base. Either that, or an autopsy of an instance of SCP-3035 that was killed while trying to leave from Entry Point B. Keep in mind, these critters are based on Mimic, a late 90s horror flick about a bug that mimics human appearances.

I'm also debating clarifying in the main article itself that while the mimicry is superficial, secondary characteristics produced by this mimicry (such as increased size, or mimicked 'stingers') still have the results you'd expect (making the bugs stronger, or giving the bugs a sharp object to stab you with).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't feel any obligation to respond, but — if you're up to it — could you elaborate? Is it the tonal issues with the emails? Some of the sillier emails thrown into the log? I did that to humanize Dr. Breenan, but in retrospect, the actual article itself would probably cut out emails irrelevant to understanding what occurred.

(Not to imply that I'm digging for all the upvotes I can get here; I'm totally fine with no-votes and down-votes. But I'm always working to improve my writing, so feedback is always super-helpful!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I experimented with moving that bit into the memo, but there's really no way to work it in that feels natural. Ultimately, after thinking about it a while, I decided to cut it — it's one of my favorite lines, but sometimes you have to throw your babies in the dumpster*. Because, yeah, I agree that it's not in-line with what you'd expect from a scientific document.

  • NOTE: Don't actually throw any babies into dumpsters >:

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair enough! I don't feel like I can take the email chain out, though — largely because it's my personal favorite part of this article, but also because (for me) it's meant as the set up as to why packages filled with test-tubes and human bodyparts keep emerging from the facility (because the bugs are mimicking scientists).

That being said, on rereading it, I can definitely see what you mean about it working without the email bits — I think you're right, and I can see why you'd prefer that as an article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot! Some of the emails are a bit on the silly side, but I could totally imagine a bored researcher sending them out. The last email is just perfect.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jesus Christ, that last email is absolutely chilling. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 from me, the last entry made me smile in a very grim, self satisfied way.

My only problem with the article is how they singlehandedly compromised an SCP site. I understand that they can mimic movements, but they can't mimic guns or flamethrowers. I feel like it would be similar to colonial marines vs. aliens, but surely a well trained task force could defeat the bugs?

Maybe add a few exploration logs (SCP 1730 comes to mind) by the failed teams, showing how the roaches responded to the threat of the task forces and got smarter? Maybe show them learning squad tactics through the creeping realizations of a doomed squad as they get slowly picked off?

Its up to you, I just feel like by itself it seems a little dicey that these guys could compromise an entire site.

Still a fantastic article, just my two cents.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is simple, but you explained it in such a way that it makes it alot more interesting. I feel like you can expand alot more on the subject. Very good my friend

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Tap-Dancing Christ, you keep putting out some good articles
I thought this would be another typical "scary bug that does scary thing" article, but this is amazing
It has the humor from the J articles, but it is just as serious a problem as any other SCP
Simple. Amazing.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd also wonder if one of those anomalies becomes the new dominant life form, thus leading to a more dangerous mimicry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do wonder what has happened to the other anomalies contained at Site-153, if any.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Paging Dr. Hellstrom, Dr. Hellstrom please report.

They're philosophical zombie cockroaches. It reminds me a little of the giant spider that mimics human voices, in that it's brilliant and disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know if I should be disgusted or altruistic, and that is very concerning.

Take my +1, you sick bastard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this, but I can't help but feel the researcher was a little careless

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nice and disgusting, but the tonal issues are keeping me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ew

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really like this, especially the last email.

But, can't bring myself to upvote while this line is in there:

To put it in the words of one Foundation soldier: "No matter how crazy they look, they're still just bugs."

I don't think the main description in an scp would include a flavor quote.

Edit: easy solution, move it into the "to whom it may concern" block.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, this is so many flavors of fucked up! That last email was priceless, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once again, the construction is clever, and this sells a simple idea well.

I'm also very impressed with your willingness to listen to feedback and make changes quickly (both here and in 3034) - and your discernment in identifying which changes are sensible.

I look forward to reading what you've got coming up next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the main description and upvoted for it, but the long e-mail chain felt redundant and went on for just long enough to make a sharp, short funny article feel repetitious and uninteresting. Everything you need to stay is stated in the main article. I did enjoy the imagery of a roach sending an email but it wasnt worth the restated setup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure I can buy that they took over an entire site. Were there really that many of them, and able to overpower whatever firepower the Foundation had on site?

I don't know. I can see the Foundation's Contain, don't Destroy policy causing this.

"We need to destroy them."
"But they're anomalous, you know we can't do that."
"Yeah, but if we wait any longer they might…"
"Look, fine. I see what you're getting at, but you'll still need to file the proper paperwork. Get the right form from John down in Filing and we'll review your request."
…
…
"CHIRP CHIRP MOTHERFUCKER"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just saw the updates, much better now. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair — I think the 'fuck you' was reasonable, but only if you actually read Xao's letter (I tried to hint at it with the subject line — Re: Where's Your Spirit Of Scientific Inquiry?). That being said, yeah, I'll tighten up on those spots you've mentioned.

(I'm also increasing the size they grow in the penultimate entry, and removing the 'fuck you' bit — but keeping the 'We're ending this now' bit).

Also, I'm glad someone caught the reference. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pretty much addressed all my concerns, and the amount of thought you've put into this while restraining information makes me immensely happy. Hope to see more articles from you.

In the meantime, take my +1 and never write something like this ever again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this but there are a few tone issues for me:
"scribbled with gibberish" and "with extreme prejudice" sound rather informal/unscientific to me.

The email where the doc says they are hesitant to mention something, doesn't make too much sense - they are Foundation researchers, they know the kinds of shit they deal with and that even the tiniest change could be relevant. I doubt they would have any issue at all mentioning a perceived size change.

The second from last email where he's suddenly all fuck this and fuck you seems to come out of nowhere, and I find it hard to believe he would react that way at all, even if they did grow significantly - at that point they're still only, what, twice the size of a normal cockroach? I dunno, felt forced to me.

I'll no-vote for now but this was pretty good. I especially enjoyed the Starship Troopers reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm — I'll take a look at that paragraph. I'm a little in love with the last line of it, but I also know that it's kind of informal, and might not be in-line with what an SCP-article is supposed to be.

Re: How they won — I went back and forth about whether or not to include this, but basically… 1) They're cockroaches, so they're both incredibly hard to kill and breed super-fast — and 2) They rapidly adapt the attributes of any creature trying to harm or kill them. So once they started encountering soldiers, they started acting like soldiers (and looking like them, too).

Like, I had struggled over whether or not to include some sort of attachment describing their takeover of the facility (or the three attempts to go into the base and rescue survivors) — but ultimately, I decided I really, really liked just ending it on the note of that final email.

That being said, I'm sure there's something I could add in the memo to indicate the two reasons I mention above (their rapid reproductive speed and their ability to adapt to their attackers).

edit: That being said, I referred to there being only 12 cockroaches in the enclosure… that's really not a lot. I've edited the document to remove the reference to 12, leaving it ambiguous (depending on the size of the enclosure, it can be presumed there were dozens, maybe even more).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Negative comments:

  • the paragraph before "to whomever it may concern" could use some tightening up on tone. It sounds more like a news article than a document at that point, and I'm sure there's a formal way to express the information in the quote.
  • I'm not sure I can buy that they took over an entire site. Were there really that many of them, and able to overpower whatever firepower the Foundation had on site?

Positive comments:

  • This is the most disgust I have felt reading an article in months. Congrats.

Settling on neutral until I can get some clarification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks — and I'm glad that's the case! Also, thanks for linking SCP-2006 — it's a great entry I hadn't seen, and it's probably a good thing I saw it now since I had a very similar idea brewing in the back of my brain. >_>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. It reminds me of SCP-2006 (in that you really don't want these fuckers to get out and start mimicking some of the awful behavior humanity has a nasty habit of exhibiting, just like you don't want 2006 to get out and discover real horror.) Aside from that, this is nice succinct little jab at the aforementioned behavior. And to top it off it made me smirk. You've made a day that started with a headache infinitely better. ;) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From my heart and from my hand
Why don't people understand
My intentions~

SOURCES:

  • Image from here.
  • Idea inspired by the 1997 film, Mimic — and Richard Feynman's talks about Cargo Cult Science.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
